data.title: Invesco Value Opportunities Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance quoted is past performance and cannot guarantee comparable future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Visit invesco.com for the most recent month-end performance. Performance figures reflect reinvested distributions and changes in net asset value (NAV). Investment return and principal value will vary so that you may have a gain or a loss when you sell shares. Returns less than one year are cumulative; all others are annualized. Performance shown prior to the inception date of Class R6 shares is that of Class A shares and includes the 12b-1 fees applicable to Class A shares. Index source: RIMES Technologies Corp. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed in the past, returns would have been lower. Performance shown at NAV does not include the applicable front-end sales charge, which would have reduced the performance.

Class Y and R6 shares have no sales charge; therefore performance is at NAV. Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. Class R6 shares are closed to most investors. Please see the prospectus for more details.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • In managing the fund, we employ an intrinsic value approach to select holdings. It should be noted that the portfolio is not constructed based on a short-term macroeconomic view. Instead, positioning is driven by a bottom-up stock selection process based on our estimate of intrinsic value.
  • We seek to create wealth by maintaining a long-term investment horizon and by investing in companies that we believe are significantly undervalued on an absolute basis.
  • Given our focus on intrinsic value and our long-term investment horizon, at quarter end, the fund was positioned with greater exposure to economically sensitive stocks compared to its peers.
  • Following the outperformance of large-cap stocks over small-cap stocks in recent years, we see greater long-term upside to intrinsic value in many small-caps compared to large-caps.
  • At quarter end, the difference between the market prices of the fund’s holdings and our estimates of their intrinsic value was attractive, in our view.
  • Although there is no assurance that market value will ever reflect our estimate of the fund’s intrinsic value, we believe the gap between price and estimated intrinsic value indicates attractive long-term capital appreciation potential.

During the quarter, tariff policy and recession risks appeared to raise investors’ short-term fears, allowing us to identify stocks with attractive long-term upside to intrinsic value across a diverse set of industries. At quarter end, the fund had overweights in more economically sensitive stocks than its benchmark and peers.

Notable Additions:

Electronic Arts (EA): The company is a leading global developer of video games, including titles such as EA Sports FC, Madden NFL and The Sims. The company provided weaker-than- expected financial guidance due to lackluster results for EA Sports FC. The near-term effect of this issue in our view created a disconnect between market price and our estimate of intrinsic value. We believe these issues are temporary in nature and should be resolved with future updates.

Microchip Technology (MCHP): The company produces a wide range of semiconductor products, including microcontrollers, analog, and mixed-signal integrated circuits that are used in automotive and industrial markets, as well as consumer end products. A downturn in auto and industrial demand has pressured the stock price, allowing us to purchase the company at what we see as a significant discount to intrinsic value. In our view, the chipmaker has a broad product line that is sold to a diverse set of end markets, customers and channels. We believe it will be well positioned when the cycle turns positive.

Notable Sales:

Leonardo (OTCPK:FINMF): The company is one of Europe’s largest defense firms and a strong free cash flow generator. A new CEO and board have achieved in our opinion meaningful operational and ROIC (return on invested capital) improvements. We sold after the stock rose on news the European Union would boost defense spending. We believe the higher share price reduced upside potential relative to our estimate of intrinsic value.

Intra-Cellular Therapies: This biopharmaceutical company discovers, develops and commercializes small molecule drugs for psychiatric and neurological disorders. In January, Johnson & Johnson (not a fund holding) made a bid to acquire it at a premium, sending shares higher. We sold the position after the announcement.

Within the fund, seven of 10 sectors had negative returns. Only materials, utilities and communication services posted positive returns. Stock selection in materials aided relative performance. Within materials, a number of the fund’s mining holdings were notable contributors. In terms of sector results, IT had the lowest absolute return, driven in part by a decline in artificial intelligence (AI)-related stocks. Selection and an overweight in IT was the primary detractor from the fund’s relative return as well.

Contributors to performance

Leonardo: This Italian aerospace and defense firm reported strong results for 2024 with double-digit increases in revenues and new orders. More broadly, Leonardo’s share price also benefited from an outlook for higher defense spending by European nations.

Agnico Eagle Mines: This Canadian gold mining company reported strong financial results for the fourth quarter and full year. The stock also benefited from an increase in gold prices during the quarter as investors appeared to move into assets perceived as “safe havens” amid volatility.

Newmont: A leading US mining company that produces copper, silver, zinc and lead, Newmont reported better-than-expected earnings and higher revenues in its latest quarterly financials. The stock also benefited from higher gold prices.

Detractors from performance Marvell Technology: Shares of this networking semiconductor company declined following news about DeepSeek, a lower cost AI model, as investors appeared to worry companies might not need to invest as heavily in AI model development. We believe concern about the impact of DeepSeek on long-term AI opportunities is misplaced.

Coherent: This laser company develops and manufactures optoelectronic components and devices. Its optical transceivers are key enablers for networking of AI servers. The stock also declined following news about DeepSeek.

Lumentum: Shares of this market-leading designer and manufacturer of innovative optical and photonic products for the communications and electronics end markets also suffered amid the broader decline in AI- related stocks.

Invesco Value Opportunities Fund Q1 2025 by Invesco US

data.title: The Future Of Mosaic: Adapting To Change In The Fertilizer Industry
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Mosaic (NYSE:MOS) is not just another company in the agricultural sector, but is a key player in the global food production chain, and is going through a transformation process that can lay the foundations for its next growth cycle.

After very volatile years in the fertilizer market – with price spikes after the pandemic, logistical disruptions and the impact of the war in Ukraine – the sector is beginning to stabilize. In this new scenario, I consider that MOS is well positioned, since it is adjusting its structure, reducing costs, investing in efficiency, and providing more sustainable solutions to producers.

Its progress is evident. Proof of this is that in Q1 2025, MOS doubled its net profits, with a strong improvement in margins and a very good performance in Brazil. The new plant in Palmeirante and the advances in operational efficiency at Mosaic Fertilizantes demonstrate solid execution.

Furthermore, the biological solutions unit Mosaic Biosciences continues to grow strongly, aligned with a long-term trend: more sustainable fertilizers with a lower environmental impact and greater added value. Neptunion, its new bioproduct launched in China, is a firm step in that direction.

MOS also appears attractive in terms of its valuation, trading at significant discounts to the sector (P/E, EV/EBITDA, P/B) and paying an above-average dividend. Although its growth metrics still reflect weakness, its balance sheet is solid, debt is manageable, and operating cash flow remains robust.

Of course, there is no shortage of risks: climate, trade policies, increasing competition, and environmental regulations are all difficult variables to control. But even within this framework, MOS has the scale, experience, and strategy to adapt and innovate.

Overview

The Mosaic Company (MOS) is one of those names that weighs in the agricultural world. Headquartered in Florida with key operations in the United States, Brazil and Canada, it is dedicated to producing two of the most important nutrients for crop growth: phosphate and potash.

But beyond that, it is a company that has earned its place by building a global network that connects land to table, from mines to fields. In 2025, MOS is going through a moment of reinvention. After a couple of intense years marked by price swings—post-pandemic, war in Ukraine, logistical cuts—the global fertilizer market is stabilizing. Prices are no longer remarkable as they used to be in the past, but there is still high demand. Also, the population keeps increasing but the available farmland is diminishing, which heightens the need to produce more food with limited resources.

And it is in this context where MOS sees a great opportunity. The company is focusing on operational efficiency, reducing costs and refining processes. But it's not just a matter of cutting costs: it is also investing in technology, automation and digital solutions for producers. Today it is no longer just about selling fertilizer, but about offering real agronomic value, with more intelligent products adapted to each type of soil.

A good example is MicroEssentials, a line that combines various nutrients in a single application and allows more efficient absorption by the crop. These types of more sophisticated solutions are key for more sustainable and productive agriculture.

In addition, MOS is betting heavily on the south of the continent. Brazil is one of its key markets, and this year the company is completing the construction of a new plant in Palmeirante, designed to improve logistics and service in one of the most dynamic agricultural regions on the planet.

The sector is also changing. On the one hand, regulatory and environmental pressure is growing. On the other, there is a wave of technological development in agriculture that is pushing producers to make better use of every gram of fertilizer. In this context, large, integrated companies that can combine scale with innovation have an advantage. And Mosaic, without a doubt, plays in that league.

According to recent projections, the global fertilizer market will continue to grow at a rapid pace in the coming years, driven by the structural demand for food and the transition toward more efficient and less polluting agriculture.

Mosaic, for its part, seems to have understood the message: adapting is not just about resisting, but about leading change. It is adjusting its operations, strengthening its presence where there is growth—such as Latin America—and focusing on the types of solutions that producers truly need.

In short, MOS is entering this new era of global agriculture with its feet firmly on the ground and its eyes set on the future. The era of fertilizer as a pure commodity is coming; what's coming is a more demanding, more digital, and more efficient agriculture. And Mosaic, with its experience, scale, and drive for innovation, seems ready for that challenge.

Q1 2025

Mosaic's net income for Q1 2025 surged to $238 MM, a staggering YoY increase of 428.9% alongside adjusted EBITDA, which is set at $544 MM (-5.6% YoY). This corresponds to the early signs of reactivation in the fertilizer market. While there was a slight dip in net sales to $2,620 B declining 2.2% YoY sales was supported by improved margins and profitability expenses.

One of the highlights was the performance of Mosaic Fertilizers, the Brazilian unit, which doubled its operating profits compared to last year and showed a strong improvement in efficiency, even despite the negative exchange rate impact. With higher sales volumes and lower conversion costs, the company expects the Q2 to be even stronger, further driven by the new blending plant in Palmeirante.

In potassium, while volumes reduced slightly and costs increased due to maintenance work, Mosaic projects production growth for the rest of the year, thanks to strong international demand.The Hydrofloat project in Esterhazy promises to reduce costs per ton by the second half of the year.

Phosphate margins recovered from revenue loss due to production variable costs recovering from shutdowns. Recovery was also because sh the Bartow and New Wales plants are back on stream with main sh heaters. Moreover, the company remains on track to reach its target of $150 MM in cost savings planned for 2025.

Lastly, biological solutions segment of Mosaic Biosciences bolstered its sales figures and expanded its product portfolio, having introduced Neptunion in China which directly supports the MOS agenda for more environmentally friendly and advanced agricultural solutions.

Valuation

MOS presents an interesting combination: although its recent performance has been affected by the decline in agricultural commodity prices and weaker global demand, current valuation multiples reflect an opportunity for investors with risk tolerance and a medium-term vision.

From a valuation perspective, MOS is at relatively attractive levels compared to the materials sector. Its P/E FWD (12.65) is 14% below the sector average, and even 28% below its own 5-year average, suggesting moderate expectations for future earnings, possibly already discounting the current cooling in margins. This pattern is repeated in other multiples such as EV/EBITDA FWD (5.80) and Price/Cash Flow FWD (5.34), both at significantly lower levels than their peers.

Furthermore, the P/B (0.81) and P/S (0.79) show discounts of 45% and 31%, respectively, compared to the sector, levels that have historically represented floors for the stock, although this does not guarantee a reversal if profitability does not improve. Added to this is a dividend yield of 2.8%, higher than the sector average and the company's historical average, which improves its total return profile.

However, the central issue is the marked deterioration in growth and profitability metrics. In the last year, net income fell more than 84%, EBIT more than 51%, and FCF fell 60%, a very pronounced contraction due to the decline in fertilizer prices after the 2022 peak and the normalization of costs in the global agricultural sector. FWD growth rates show no signs of a quick recovery: adjusted EPS is expected to fall another 11% in 2025, and FCF growth is expected to remain negative.

Operating margins are also under pressure. The current EBITDA margin is 15.8%, 26% below its 5-year average, and the net margin is barely above 1.5%, well below the industry standard. These figures suggest that Mosaic is not only selling less, but also with lower profitability.

However, its balance sheet is not in an alarming situation. The debt level is manageable relative to the company's size (net debt/EV around 33%), and the company still generates more than $1.3B in operating cash flow, giving it room to sustain capex, cover dividends, and eventually repurchase shares if the environment improves.

Risks

MOS has several fronts to oversee that include external factors that are flat-out beyond their control.

One of the key challenges is the agriculture-based market’s demand for fertilizers. The need for harvests may decrease during agricultural slumps, which affects sales on a more dire level.

Another challenge is reliance on specific markets which is an issue. Trade policies from major nation states are a mixed bag set-into stone firewall tools. Changes in one crucial country’s trade policy can provide unforeseen hurdles that ramp up costs, or in the worst case, blockade the markets completely. Not to mention the ever-changing landscape of rules that can lead to ripper gaps in policy coverage.

Sure, competition will also be an issue, and though Mosaic enjoys a favorable outlook, it’s not the sole owner of the market. New competitors join the ranks with every passing day, competing for the companies margins.

On top of all of this, newest policies will without a doubt come into play…the ones that focus on the changing environment which in turn moves passed traditional market regulations and mandated ways to make fertilizers, raising costs.

To summarize, the weather, national policies, and competition put a customer split sign on fees add to the and external factors beyond the companies control mark keeping track of time.

Conclusions

I consider that Mosaic is an interesting bet for investors with a medium to long term vision. It is not risk-free, but its fundamentals are improving, its valuation is reasonable and its strategy is clearly aligned with modern agricultural trends.

Today, MOS doesn't just sell fertilizers. It is helping to redesign how the food of the future is produced. And for me, that's a good place to be. My recommendation is to buy, with patience and a strategic eye.

data.title: Toyota: Roughly $7.4 Billion Tariff Headwind Is Material
data.description:
Toyota Motor Corporation (TM) reported the company’s fiscal 2025 earnings. The fourth quarter showed better resilience than previous ones, as Toyota’s earnings have continued to normalize from a very strong FY2024. The FY2026 outlook suggests further earnings declines based on foreign exchange headwinds, but also on weakening underlying earnings. Tariffs add significant further uncertainty as Toyota hasn’t yet quantified the 25% automotive tariff’s impact past May.

In my initial November 2024 article on the stock, “Toyota Earnings: Production Halts And EV Concerns Justify Cautious Valuation”, I initiated Toyota at a neutral Hold rating. Since, the stock has returned 6%, slightly better than S&P 500’s return of -4%.

Toyota’s Q4 Was More Resilient Than Previous Quarters

Toyota’s performance for the fiscal fourth quarter was quite good. The company’s sales volumes improved by 10.0% year-on-year with an especially good performance in Japan and other parts of Asia, while other markets stayed stable. Compared to the full-year FY2025 performance, where sales volumes declined -0.9% based on weakness in North America and Europe, the improvement was significant.

The better volume performance was reflected in Toyota’s Q4 financials as well. Revenues grew by 11.7% year-on-year to ¥12.36 trillion, being a notable improvement from the previous two quarters’ nearly stagnant growth. Increased expenses and material costs still weighed on profitability, causing the Q4 operating margin to decline by 100 basis points to 9.0%, but operating income stayed stable at ¥1.12 trillion due to improved sales volumes. Compared to previous quarters in FY2025, Q4 showed much better margin resilience, ending the fiscal year on a relatively strong note. The whole fiscal year’s earnings decline represents normalization after extraordinarily strong FY2024 earnings from new vehicle sales.

FY2026 Outlook: Earnings Normalization & $7.4 Billion in Rough Automotive Tariff Impact

Toyota’s outlook includes further earnings pressure from previous highs. The company anticipates FY2026 operating income to end up at ¥3800 billion, down significantly from ¥4796 billion in FY2025. The earnings outlook is weak despite 9.8 million expected vehicle sales, up 4.7% year-on-year, and ¥48.5 trillion in expected revenues, up 1.0% year-on-year. Vehicle sales volumes are expected to show modest growth in other markets, but especially good growth in North America after Toyota’s four-month Indiana plant shutdown in FY2025.

Most of the decline in expected earnings is still caused by changes in foreign exchange rates, where a weakening US dollar alone is causing ¥400 billion of a total ¥745 billion foreign exchange headwind in expected operating income. Increased material prices, human resources, and other expense increases are expected to drive the rest of the FY2026 earnings decline. The earnings pressure is only offset in part by cost reductions, other savings, and sales volume growth.

I believe that excluding foreign exchange rates, the FY2026 outlook still shows quite good underlying resilience amid increasing global macroeconomic uncertainty. The sales outlook is suggested to remain stable despite rising global industry concerns, where for example Fitch recently downgraded the global automotive sector’s 2025 outlook to deteriorating from neutral. Toyota’s market positioning in more affordable cars should make the company more resilient to consumer weakness than more expensive competition, being especially important as consumer confidence has recently plummeted in the US, Japan, and in many other Toyota’s important end markets. Rising recessionary worries and tariff uncertainty can still cause pressure on Toyota's performance.

At the same time, Toyota’s FY2026 could face challenges beyond the given earnings guidance. Trump’s 25% automotive tariff is causing significant industry uncertainty, and while Trump is reportedly planning automotive tariff reliefs, the 25% tariff on foreign automotive imports has remained in place for now. Toyota’s FY2026 guidance only includes the estimated tariff impact on earnings for April and May, being ¥180 billion in size for the two months alone. In a simplified calculation, the full annual tariff impact could add up to ¥1080 billion – around $7.4 billion - prior to better tariff mitigation strategies, of which ¥900 million isn’t currently included in the ¥3800 billion FY2026 operating income guidance.

Ultimately, Toyota should be able to shift supply chains, improve domestic sourcing in the US, and pass increased costs onto consumers relatively well. I don’t expect that the final impact of tariffs will be quite the full estimated amount, but tariff uncertainty does weigh significantly on Toyota despite the company’s significant manufacturing footprint in the US. On the other hand, other tariffs are likely to weigh on material expenses past the automotive tariff’s direct impact, unless exceptions are made for materials such as steel and aluminum.

Updated Valuation

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Toyota’s stock. Given the FY2026 outlook and increasing uncertainty around the economy and tariffs, I expect EBIT to decline noticeably to ¥3350 billion for the fiscal year, including a ¥450 million tariff impact in addition to Toyota’s current guidance for the year. Afterwards, I expect Toyota’s tariff mitigation, operative initiatives, and the potential for tariff relief to drive the EBIT margin to a sustained 8.5% level. The expectation is within Toyota’s historical range.

I still expect the sales performance to be stable, stabilizing at slow 1% perpetual growth in Yen. The cash flow conversion outlook is still worsened by Toyota’s significant need for investments.

I have again subtracted non-finance segment interest expenses from cash flow, and don’t subtract debt from the estimated fair enterprise value.

The estimates put Toyota’s fair value estimate at $164.30, 13% below the stock price at the time of writing; given tariff uncertainty, I believe that Toyota’s stock is now quite unattractive. Tariff reliefs could make Toyota attractive, but for now, the policy environment makes the investment too volatile given the potential reward. The estimated overvaluation still falls within a fair margin of error.

The fair value estimate is down from $208.62 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of equity of 9.64%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don’t factor in Toyota’s debt in my cost of capital estimate. For cost of equity, I use Japan’s 10-year bond yield of 1.33% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 5.27% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for Japan. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.52. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 9.64%.

Takeaway

Toyota’s Q4 results were quite good, as volume growth made earnings resilient. On the other hand, earnings are guided to continue normalizing in FY2026 from a strong FY2024, and rising macroeconomic uncertainty could cause further turbulence for the year. Toyota only guides the automotive tariff’s impact for April and May, suggesting an annual impact of up to $7.4 billion; tariffs also add to an uncertain operating environment. For now, the stock is quite unattractive, but I remain with a Hold rating for TM.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DexCom Q1 Earnings: Organic Revenue Growth Versus Cost Pressure
data.description:
I am updating my ongoing analysis on DexCom, Inc. (NASDAQ:DXCM) in light of Q1 2025 earnings, which were released on Thursday, May 1st, 2025. I previously rated DexCom a buy for the following reasons:

  • The market overreacted. Despite a reduction in guidance, management still expected double-digit growth with no change in profitability levels.
  • DCF analysis suggested a price target of $94.30, a 26% upside to pricing at the time.
  • Demographic tailwinds on both consumer and insurer demand were too strong to ignore.

Since then, DexCom has returned 19% while the S&P 500 has returned 7%.

While I continue to see strong growth potential, I believe the cost equation may have changed, pressuring margins over the long term. This downward impact on my price target, alongside a rise in the share price, has reduced the margin of safety to less than 10%. In addition, I feel that the risk from cost pressure and the reward from outsized growth are largely balanced at the moment, and investors should look for additional information to process.

With the above in mind and a price target of $92.90, 9% upside from today's pricing, I lower my rating from buy to hold.

DexCom Q2 Earnings Review

DexCom released earnings on May 1st, reporting EPS of $0.32 (missing by $0.01) and revenue of $1.04B, beating by $18.6M.

While organic revenue accelerated, total revenue and profitability decreased across the board; gross margin declines were especially concerning.

The business reaffirmed revenue, operating margin, and EBITDA margin but lowered gross profit margin. Q1 trended slightly below guidance, so management is signaling that they expect an improvement in future quarters. That remains to be seen based on the deceleration in gross margin.

While organic revenue met, or even slightly exceeded, my expectations, cost pressures have mounted faster than expected, increasing the potential for downside.

DXCM Stock Valuation

I ran a DCF analysis incorporating the new guidance and market trends. Here are the assumptions I used:

  • 12% near-term revenue growth based on near-term market CAGR, low-end management guidance, and historical performance
  • 4% long-run growth rate heading back to market CAGR to account for competition, gross-margin challenges, and diminishing returns as the business scales
  • 7.1% discount rate based on estimated WACC

This DCF analysis yields a price target of $92.90, down from my prior target of $94.30, and a 9% upside to today's prices.

Wall Street is slightly more bullish at a price target of $97.68, noting the range is weighted towards the low-end at $82.

The quant rating is consistent with my assessment, noting an F for valuation multiples and consensus revisions, offset by strong growth and profitability relative to peers.

Demographic Tailwinds Continue To Drive Upside Potential

DexCom is a leader in a rapidly growing market with highly favorable demographic tailwinds. Starting with diabetes care devices in isolation, the market is expected to grow at a CAGR of 12.22% through at least 2029.

Beyond the current expectations for diabetes care devices, the eligible population is expected to grow rapidly. Mature markets such as North America and Europe will continue to see double-digit growth, while developing regions such as South America, Africa, and Asia will see anywhere from 50% to 134% in diabetes prevalence.

And this only reflects diabetes prevalence. DexCom is also working on tools and devices for prediabetes and diabetes prevention. This over-the-counter market is expected to grow to over $5 billion over the next 10 years, a billion more than DexCom's total revenue last year.

In addition to consumer demand, health insurers are going to become more and more motivated to support continuous glucose monitoring.

Health insurers' medical costs are expected to grow at the highest rate since the early 2010s, driven by GLP-1 drugs and acute inpatient/outpatient utilization.

This trend benefits DexCom in two ways. First, GLP-1 drugs for treating diabetes are being approved for weight loss, rapidly driving up costs. This spending nearly doubled from 2021 to 2022 alone. DexCom reduces the need for GLP-1 and insulin over time through monitoring and supporting lifestyle changes.

Second, continuous glucose monitors have been proven to reduce overall medical costs for managing diabetes by lowering the rate of inpatient and outpatient acute care, the troubling trend noted above.

Cost Pressures Present Downside Risk

On the downside, gross margin sticks out like a sore thumb in the earnings package.

The gross margin pressure flows through to the bottom line, as R&D is only up slightly, and S, G, and A are actually favorable.

Understandably, this was a major topic during the earnings call. Management struck a positive tone, but there are cracks in the cost strategy. Here are key comments on the gross margin pressure:

As we continue to rebuild our inventory levels while addressing increasing demand, we have factored in an additional 100 basis points impact to our global freight costs to support expedited shipping. We expect that this impact will lessen as we move throughout the year.

We have also built into this gross margin guidance a 50-basis-point impact of inflationary pressures from tariffs in the supply chain.

Finally, we anticipate an approximately 25 basis point impact on our global manufacturing costs based on fluctuations in the U.S. dollar.

In light of global changes to the supply chain and tariffs, these adjustments strike me as optimistic. While DexCom does have US manufacturing facilities, these facilities are under FDA notice for issues in the manufacturing process and quality management system. This, on top of tariffs, represents a significant cost risk, even if management is downplaying it.

Guidance essentially requires cost pressures to ease almost immediately and trend in a favorable direction across the year. Between trade challenges and supply chain challenges, it is unlikely that all of the pressure will ease on the timeline management needs.

Verdict

DexCom is a strong and growing company, with accelerating organic revenue growth that is actually exceeding my previous expectations. Demographic trends are heavily in DexCom's favor, with increasing demand from both consumers and insurers.

On the flip side, gross margin pressure is having an impact on cash flow and the bottom line, and I believe management is being optimistic about the rate of improvement.

With the recent run-up in price, my price target of $92.90 is only 9% up from today's prices and not a sufficient margin of safety against downside risks. With balanced upside and downside, I lower my rating on DexCom from buy to hold.

data.title: Zurich Insurance: More Growth Ahead, Buy Confirmed
data.description:
Last time, we still believed there was room for stock price growth, and since then, the stock has indeed appreciated, validating our conviction. In the meantime, Zurich Insurance Group (OTCQX:ZURVY) (OTCQX:ZFSVF) paid a generous dividend (in CHF), and we are up by 17.18% on a total return basis (Fig. 1), with a supportive performance vs. a negative S&P 500 change. In Fiscal Year 2024, the Swiss insurance group posted record earnings, setting the pace for the new 2025 plan. The company was already A Buy Ahead Of The Capital Market Day. However, we still believe in 1) a supportive pricing dynamic for the industry, 2) lower-than-expected natural catastrophe losses, and 3) downside protection thanks to a tasty dividend, a potential new buyback, and a safe Solvency Ratio.

Fig 1

Q1 Results and Our Positive Take

The company delivered a solid set of numbers in Q1, with P&C premiums meeting guidance despite rate softening, and the new Life business growth was strong. Farmers' premiums continue to recover, and the company recorded a stronger surplus ratio, implying that the division made an underwriting profit despite the California Wildfire loss. In numbers, P&C sales increased by 5% year-on-year (Fig. 2), reporting that the North America growth, excluding the latest Travel acquisition, was just 1%. This might be seen as a low number, but there are strategic management actions to increase value over volume. On the L&H, Zurich Insurance recorded an impressive Present value of new business premiums (PVNBP) of $5.1 billion, which grew by 27% year-on-year. Related to the balance sheet, the company reported a Solvency II ratio of 256%, materially higher than the Wall Street consensus expectation of 250%.

Source: Zurich Q1 press release - Fig 2

Why are we still positive?

  1. Starting with the company’s strong solvency ratio and the overall solidity of its balance sheet, we increased our confidence in a potential buyback announcement. Usually, the H1 Solvency Ratio is lower on a seasonal basis due to the dividend payment. That said, the company recorded impressive results backed by its operating cash flow generation. Therefore, we now assume at least CHF 1 billion in share repurchases, which is complementary to its tasty dividend yield. Zurich's yield is at 4.7% and is appealing in CHF terms (compared to the Swiss 10-year bond yield at 55 basis points). Additionally, Zurich’s low-risk profile is also reflected in its exposure to illiquid non-investment-grade private credit, representing <3% of its total investments in credit.
  2. Overall, the company's 2024 results were most notable for clarity on LA Wildfire exposures. Because it was a devastating event from a human perspective, we should report that the company's impact was manageable. Indeed, the Zurich division Farmers (the number 2 market share in California homeowners’ insurance) delivers solid results in Q1 2025 and is expected to remain above the top end of its target capital surplus. With a Farmers Management Services increase of 5%, we forecast an operating profit CAGR of at least 6% until 2027. In number, with Zurich's Farmers surplus ratio beating estimates, the company increased its surplus by two basis points. This implies an underwriting profit in Q1 despite the California wildfires. This shows a strong foundation for growth and higher confidence in our future estimates.
  3. More importantly, Zurich reported strong results in the L&H thanks to new business premiums. On a positive note, the segment results were also driven by protecting product growth and higher cost savings. Despite being already positive ahead of the CMD day, at the presentation, the company said that a) pricing in North America commercial lines is set to remain stable, and b) U.S. reinvestment yields are so high that investment income in non-life is likely to rise in future years. Even if there is more evidence of rate softening, the FED maintained the interest rate unchanged. Secondly, rates in commercial were +3% year-on-year and +5% in growth in the retail space.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Following the CMD release and given the ambitious 3-year targeted EPS and RoE, our core earnings per share forecast was set at 9% growth. This moved our EBIT to $8.6 billion, with a net profit projection of $6.6 billion in 2025 (EPS at CHF 44). There is M&A upside to price, but we believe Zurich will reinvest its excess capital into growth, continuing to fuel its EPS. Considering the Q1 results and our positive view, we believe choosing growth over cash is new for sell-side analysts who are used to viewing the industry as a cash yield sector rather than as a growth sector. At the time of writing, the company's share price reflects its premium valuation, but there is still upside to Zurich’s 2027 profit growth forecast. Applying lower outstanding shares due to a potential buyback, we reduce the share from 146.36 million to 144.64 million. Even with no change in our net profit forecast (and we believe the company will manage to outperform a 9% growth), our EPS moved to CHF 45.68. Continuing to apply an unchanged P/E of 13.5x, our valuation reached CHF 616 per share ($37.25 in ADR).

Risks

Investors in Zurich Group are exposed to a wide variety of risks including: 1) changes in financial markets (public equity and fixed income solution), indeed, any changes might impact the company's profitability and its solvency II ratio, 2) higher corporate taxes, 3) higher-than-anticipated Nat Cat losses, cyber risk, terrorist attack (as an example the LA wildfire is just the latest adverse event with a $200 million impact on the P&L), 4) changes in currency development, Zurich reports in USD, but it is listed in CHF, 5) accounting changes, and higher regulatory requirements. Furthermore, Zurich might be exposed to M&A risks given the strong balance sheet.

Conclusion

The company has been one of the industry's steadiest and most consistent performers. We believe Zurich's EPS growth story still needs to be priced in with its appealing yield (particularly in CHF terms). While we see 10% total return potential in the next twelve months, we also see upside in Swiss RE.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nebius: Recent Nosedive Is A Golden Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

My February 2025 bullish Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) recommendation did not age well. The stock has nosedived by almost 50% over the last three months, which is a poor performance. However, the stock does not equal the company. What I mean is that it is quite common when the share price plunges over the short-term despite fortifying fundamentals. The stock is an absolute steal after such a deep pullback, meaning that I am reiterating my 'Strong buy' recommendation.

Fundamental analysis

The artificial intelligence revolution continues accumulating its momentum, which is the biggest secular bullish catalyst for NBIS. According to Grand View Research, the European AI market is expected to reach $370 billion by 2030. This is more than five times larger compared to the market size in 2024.

Amid such a rapidly growing market, NBIS has great chances to absorb the massive positive effect of industry tailwinds. There is an apparent competitive advantage, which is the full-stack AI infrastructure of Nebius. The company builds a diversified ecosystem of AI-centric offerings, which certainly increases the company's cross-selling potential.

According to the Q4 earnings call, Nebius is aggressively driving and innovation growth across the entire ecosystem. For example, the autonomous technology platform (Avride) signed contracts with Uber and Grubhub and expanded operations in Japan. Toloka (AI data partner) has demonstrated a 140% year-over-year revenue growth in Q4 2024, and TripleTen (EdTech) doubled new student additions in 2024.

The management is not resting on their laurels, as they continue to invest aggressively in growth projects. In early March, the company announced a major upgrade to its US-based cloud computing capacity with the planned construction of a new data center in New Jersey with capacity of up to 300 MW. The company also continues expanding its technological partnership with Nvidia as Nebius was among the early adopters of Nvidia Blackwell family of AI accelerators.

While being an early adopter of Nvidia's accelerators is already a big strategic advantage, the management works really hard to diversify Nebius' partnership network. Just a week ago, a partnership with DDN, one of the leading companies in AI and data intelligence solutions, was reported. The partnership aims to deliver advanced performance for AI workloads, which is crucial to deliver more value to customers.

I also like that Nebius is quite aggressive in its geographic expansion. The company plans to expand its AI infrastructure in Asia as well, with Singapore being considered as the major hub. This looks like a sound move because the AI market in Asia is expected to grow much faster compared to Europe, with a projected CAGR of almost 40% by 2033.

The company's balance sheet is nothing but a fortress as Nebius ended FY2024 with a vast $2.5 billion cash cushion and less than $300 million in total liabilities. With that being said, Nebius boasts massive financial power and flexibility. Therefore, we can expect new promising partnership or expansion announcements in the near future.

I think that with such a strong cash position and almost no liabilities, Nebius can make very bold growth moves, which might include acquisitions as well. I think that with all these positives, it is highly likely that there will be bold forward-looking plans and announcements shared during the upcoming earnings release (scheduled for May 20).

Valuation analysis

The market cap of NBIS is currently $5.9 billion, which is quite modest if compared to the management's forecast of a circa $1 billion ARR by the end of this year.

Nebius has almost no debt on the face of its balance sheet, meaning that the DCF model can be discounted using the cost of equity. The discount rate for NBIS is 7.85%, according to my calculations.

Since we saw that the AI momentum remains strong, I use the same 5% constant growth rate to calculate the company's terminal value ('TV'). This assumption is the trickiest as many experts suggest that it is not realistic for a company to maintain a growth rate that will be higher than GDP growth over a long period of time.

However, there are various real-life examples. Companies like Microsoft and Apple have been maintaining a 15%+ revenue CAGR since the 1980s. Therefore, my 5% constant growth rate assumption appears to be fairly conservative. The big reason why a company can by far outperform the broader economy's growth for decades is the GDP's structure.

According to IMF, 36% of the U.S. GDP is represented by government expenditure which means it is by far the largest contributor to the country's economy. The U.S. Federal government spending was at around $6.75 trillion in 2024, while Federal R&D budget was around $200 billion. This is around 3% of the total, while technological companies usually reinvest into innovation much larger portions of their revenues because it is the cornerstone of their growth strategies. It is also vital to keep in mind that corporations have an opportunity to drive 'inorganic' growth via M&A activity, which is impossible for governments.

Revenue assumptions for FY2026-2027 were obtained from consensus. For the horizon after FY2027, I used a 32.5% revenue CAGR. Expanding its potential to capitalize on AI tailwinds requires intensive capital spending, meaning that the FCF margin is poised to be thin within the next couple of years. Therefore, I used a subtle 3% FCF margin for FY2026-2027. However, once NBIS finishes its data center buildout, the company will highly likely be able to boost profitability growth due to its cross-selling potential. Therefore, I expect the FCF margin to expand by 300-basis points yearly in FY2028-2030.

Since Seeking Alpha suggests that there are 235.8 million NBIS shares outstanding, the fair share price is roughly $57. That being said, the upside potential is a massive 126%. The average target price from Wall Street analysts is more conservative at $41. Nevertheless, the upside potential is still large at 65%. Moreover, the current share price of around $25 is lower than the lowest price estimate from Wall Street.

Mitigating factors

The competition is fierce. Nebius competes almost directly with behemoths like Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. All these players possess much larger financial resources and human capital. I think that this one is the biggest fundamental risk for Nebius. On the other hand, Nebius is managed by Arkady Volozh, the CEO, who was quite successful in building a solid European search engine player, Yandex. This company remains quite successful despite Google's dominance in the space.

The highly uncertain environment is also a large headwind for all growth stocks. The uncertainty does not only relate to geopolitics and tariffs but also to the monetary policy of the Fed and European Central Bank. In such an environment, markets are becoming more nervous, and any unfavorable headline might lead to a stock sell-off, especially when we speak about young under-the-radar names like Nebius.

From the share price perspective, the setup looks quite questionable as well. Experiencing a 50% drawdown within less than a quarter might spook investors and significantly undermine their confidence.

Conclusion

I think that after almost a 50% plunge over the last three months, NBIS is a no-brainer 'Strong buy'. This is a high-quality company that has great potential to exercise solid cross-selling power amid the rapidly accelerating AI momentum.

data.title: Revvity: Despite Facing Multiple Headwinds, Shares Are A Buy
data.description:
It's been an absolutely dismal couple of years for the life sciences industry. It seems like since 2023, we've always been six months away from an industry recovery. We've seen numerous reports of purported green shoots for the life sciences space, but that new dawn has never actually arrived.

Revvity (NYSE:RVTY), formerly known as PerkinElmer, had held up better than some of its life sciences peers during this downturn. However, it too has given way over the past quarter, with shares once again approaching five-year lows. Shares are now down more than 50% from the highs set at the beginning of 2022:

The decline has continued into 2025, with RVTY stock trading down 16% year-to-date and missing out entirely on the recent market rebound.

It's not hard to see why investors are frustrated with life sciences companies in general and Revvity in particular.

Revvity Faces A Perfect Storm Of Negativity

These companies tend to rely heavily on pharma and biotech to generate revenues. A core business for life sciences companies is selling various tools and services for drug development. Revvity, for its part, gets about 35% of its revenues from biotech and pharma as per its most recent conference call.

Unfortunately, biotech has been in a brutal run of its own:

Over the past decade, the SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI) is up a mere 12%. That's against a 119% gain for the healthcare sector, a 218% return for the S&P 500, and a nearly 400% run in the Nasdaq 100. Understandably, investors are growing skeptical about putting more money into clinical stage biotech firms given the dismal returns from the sector for many years now.

Biotech does better with lower interest rates. It takes many years to bring a new drug from the ideation stage up through trials and on to commercial launch. That long wait time is more tolerable when the cost of capital is very low, but as interest rates have surged, investors have instead prioritized sectors with faster payback times.

In addition, the artificial intelligence boom has caused many venture capital firms to emphasize their investments in AI while reducing or entirely stopping their efforts in sectors such as biotech where recent returns have been lackluster.

Biotech has taken a fresh leg downward over the past six months:

That's in large part due to political uncertainty. Investors are skeptical about the Trump Administration and key figures such as Robert F. Kennedy Jr. We could see major changes in the regulatory landscape for new drugs and vaccines.

We saw another reflection of this on Tuesday when Vinay Prasad was named the head of the FDA's Center for Biologics Evaluation and Research ("CBER"). Prasad is known for outspoken and often critical views on various parts of the drug and vaccine development process. Shares of the S&P Biotech ETF (XBI) fell nearly 7% on Tuesday in reaction to the Prasad news, and shares of many vaccine and drug developers were down double-digits on the headlines. This highlights the perceived higher risk of deploying capital to the biotech and life sciences field given current regulatory uncertainty.

Revvity's pharma and biotech revenue stream is particularly tilted towards preclinical and early stage drug development. That's where activity has dropped off the most quickly.

Understandably, if a drug is already far along in trials, companies are more likely to carry it through to fruition despite the negative economic and regulatory headwinds today. But, it's much easier to cancel preclinical or Phase I work on drugs as there is less sunk cost there.

Revvity faces another headwind here as well. That's because it has significant exposure to animal-based clinical trials through its In Vivo Imaging business. This provides services for preclinical animal trials. The FDA recently announced a long-term plan that aims to eventually phase out animal trials for new drug development. Companies such as Revvity which have animal testing-related revenues could see a meaningful drag from this shift going forward.

Pharma and biotech research isn't the only concerning part of Revvity's business right now. Another issue is with the U.S. academic market.

The Trump Administration has announced significant changes to funding for the clinical research at academic institutions. The National Institutes of Health ("NIH") plans to give less funding to research institutions going forward. This is tied up in courts for the time being, and from my understanding, prior funding levels remain in effect until more legal clarity is established. Regardless, many universities are slashing their research budgets to hunker down in case the NIH cuts end up becoming the new standard.

To be clear, this NIH funding issue is a problem across the entirety of the life sciences space; it's hardly specific to Revvity. Indeed, Revvity generates a modest 5% of its total revenues from U.S. academic institutions. You could reasonably argue that perhaps sentiment is worse here than the actual business impact. Regardless, it will be a drag on 2025 results, and perhaps be a longer-term structural reduction to growth as well.

Revvity has another longer-term concern which is quite specific to its own operations. Namely, one of its strongest lines of business is in newborn screening. Revvity offers testing for a variety of things such as rare diseases and genetic conditions.

Revvity has been a leader in the newborn testing space for decades; it's one of the company's calling cards. Alas, global fertility rates have been in dramatic decline. This was already a concern in recent years, and global fertility appears to have decelerated even more over the past five years. Previously, many investors had been under the idea that falling fertility rates were mostly a developed market issue. However, over the past five to ten years we've seen that birth rates are also slumping in Latin America, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia among other regions. As a result, the total addressable market for Revvity's newborn testing services is likely to dramatically decrease over time.

To Revvity's credit, it is a dominant competitor in this space. So far, it has been able to stave off revenue decline from this division due to having pricing power and taking share from competitors. However, investors should realize that Revvity is going to be swimming upstream in terms of trying to grow that business as the number of newborn children around the world continues to decline.

As if that weren't enough, Revvity has another pressing matter at the moment. That would be the tariffs.

In its Q1 conference call, Revvity stated that the impact from tariffs would have been about $135 million this year if it did not make changes to soften the blow. However, the company says that it started preparing for tariff impacts months ago. As such, it hopes to be able to avoid a significant chunk of that headline $135 million. On a related note, the company is engaged in additional cost-cutting measures to further reduce the hit to margins from tariffs.

As for addressing tariff impacts directly, Revvity is moving some goods production out of the U.S. to avoid other countries' reciprocal tariffs against America. For example, Revvity has a significant Chinese business. It already produces much of its goods for the Chinese market within China and it can move other production to Europe so as to avoid direct U.S.-China trade barriers.

Surprisingly enough, Revvity still plans to maintain flat to slightly up revenues from China this year despite the trade war. On the one hand, the relocation of production makes sense and this mitigation strategy seems plausible. However, it's not hard to imagine a scenario where China reduces business ties with U.S. companies more broadly. If the tariffs stay in place, Revvity could lose sales regardless of where it manufactures its products.

Why Revvity Is A Buy Despite All The Headwinds

I went into considerable detail around the negatives that Revvity is facing right now. Sentiment for both the sector as a whole and Revvity are at depressed levels. And that's not without good cause.

But, that's baked into the stock price and then some with the stock down more than 50% off the highs.

So, let's turn to the positives. The stock is cheap compared to its historical averages.

There are one-time items which distort a chart of P/E. However, for EV/EBITDA, RVTY stock is back down to 15x. That's near the low end of its 10-year range, with shares trading for as much as 25x during more upbeat times:

And, intriguingly enough, despite all the challenges, the company's earnings are hanging in there.

Revvity's Q1 results, released April 28th, actually beat on both the top- and bottom lines. More importantly, the company maintained its full year revenue and earnings guidance despite the hit from tariffs. Of note, it appears that strength from Revvity's small but rapidly growing software business will be enough to offset weakness from tariffs and reduced academic spending.

It wasn't just software either. A couple other business lines including the newborn testing division showed better than expected strength. Finally, the company has been buying back stock aggressively over the past year, so that helps support earnings per share as well.

One potential concern with Revvity has been its fairly high degree of leverage. The company has often operated at 3x net debt to EBITDA, but this has dipped to 2.5x now following a recent asset sale along with some paying down of debt. Given the tougher industry and broad economic conditions, it's good to see the efforts to clean up the balance sheet a bit. Regardless, the company still employs plenty of leverage meaning that when market conditions improve, there should be a good deal of torque in the stock price.

Revvity Bottom Line

It might seem surprising that Revvity's results were as strong as they were in Q1. However, that speaks to how long the life sciences sector has been in a slump. The industry has been in an historic bust since 2023 when COVID-19 related testing and vaccine development revenues dried up along with being when biotechs starting to lose access to funding for new trials. After almost two years of terrible earnings, it's hard for results to get much worse than they already are barring a complete calamity.

So it's not too hard for a firm like Revvity to be able to at least maintain its prior level of earnings. The bar here to beat expectations is quite low. It's possible that shares of Revvity and the industry as a whole could move even lower amid tariffs, the changing regulatory environment, and other such concerns. That said, just look at this chart of the industry-wide landscape since 2022:

When earnings stop dropping and companies can maintain guidance and yet the stock prices are all acting like this, it's generally a fine buying opportunity.

RVTY shares look compelling in isolation. That said, I have a buy rating on the stock rather than a strong buy. And I do not personally own any Revvity just yet.

The reason is that the whole industry is in a similar slump. So, you really have your pick of which companies and product verticals that you want to get exposure to.

To give context here, consider that Morningstar rates Revvity as a 5-star stock. It assigns a fair value of $162 for RVTY shares, which implies more than 40% undervaluation at today's price. That said, currently Morningstar also sees Danaher (DHR), Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO), Agilent (A), and Ilumina (ILMN) as being at deep discounts to fair value as well.

My contention is that the whole industry has gotten overly punished for the last two years of bad news across the space. I personally would be fine with allocating funds to Revvity tomorrow. I expect its shares to trade significantly higher five years from now.

That said, there are other names in the sector, like Bruker (BRKR) or Danaher that I prefer due to seeing more long-term tailwinds in their end markets.

By contrast, several of Revvity's markets seem somewhat challenged in the bigger picture. That would include the newborn testing space along with the company's imaging operations related to live animals. At a time when there are so many life sciences stocks on sale, I'm personally skipping ones which face longer-term demand issues. That said, RVTY shares are certainly cheap here and merit a buy rating even if there are peers I like better for now.
